angle.headline: Why Your T-MEC Strategy Needs a Strategist, Not Another Lawyer
angle.thesis: Most US-LATAM corridor businesses treat trade negotiations like a compliance exercise when the real value is in designing the operating model that can flex across three regulatory systems without breaking.
angle.targetPersona: Mid-market manufacturers and distributors ($10M-$100M) operating cross-border supply chains between the US and Mexico, particularly those with exposure to automotive, steel, or agricultural sectors affected by ongoing T-MEC review
angle.relevanceToAudience: Mexico is entering T-MEC renegotiations with a technical team focused on production regionalization while US businesses continue paying tariffs on products that should qualify for preferential treatment — the gap is not legal knowledge, it's operational architecture that can prove origin compliance without manual heroics
talkingPoints: Mexican exporters continue paying US tariffs on T-MEC-eligible products because their supply chains cannot systematically prove rules of origin compliance — this is not a documentation problem, it is an operating model problem.

Mexico's government is deploying financial technicians (Lazzeri) instead of diplomats to the T-MEC negotiation because they understand what US mid-market owners missed: trade strategy is supply chain architecture, not tariff code lookup.

The pattern mirrors tech regret: 74% of US SMBs are re-evaluating software purchases because they bought solutions before they had the operating model to absorb them — trade compliance tools fail the same way when deployed into manual processes.

The concrete action is not hiring a customs broker — it is mapping your physical production flow to identify which inputs can be regionally sourced and then redesigning procurement SOPs so origin compliance becomes automatic, not heroic.

enContent.subject: Why your last software buy didn't stick
enContent.preheader: The tech regret cycle is an operating model problem, not a vendor problem.
subjectOptions: Why your last software buy didn't stick
Three-quarters of owners regret their last tech buy
The operating-model tax on every AI purchase

sections.heading: The Signal
sections.body:
*This week: the mid-market is discovering that every tech buy is actually an operating-model test, and most are failing it.*

- **Strategy:** [Chief Executive](https://chiefexecutive.net/preparing-for-uncertainty-without-slowing-progress/) argues that manufacturers already have the tools they need to navigate uncertainty, and that what they lack is a framework. **Strategy is not the tools you own. It is the rule that tells you which one to use.** [Read in Chief Executive Magazine ->](https://chiefexecutive.net/preparing-for-uncertainty-without-slowing-progress/)
- **Operating Models:** According to [HR Executive](https://hrexecutive.com/how-hr-can-break-the-tech-regret-cycle/), citing Expert Market research, nearly three-quarters of US SMBs are re-evaluating current tech investments, with poor organizational fit as the primary cause of regret. **The operating model is what the business knew how to do before the software arrived.** [Read in HR Executive ->](https://hrexecutive.com/how-hr-can-break-the-tech-regret-cycle/)
- **Technology:** Instacart co-founder Apoorva Mehta launched a hedge fund that relies on AI agents to replace fundamental portfolio managers, per [Bloomberg](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-24/instacart-co-founder-debuts-hedge-fund-where-ai-calls-the-shots). **Technology only replaces a job when that job was already a repeatable decision.** [Read in Bloomberg ->](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-24/instacart-co-founder-debuts-hedge-fund-where-ai-calls-the-shots)
- **Human Capital:** [Chief Executive](https://chiefexecutive.net/to-survive-ai-your-employees-must-evolve-asap/) frames the AI era as a mandate for employees to become value creators through AI-augmented insight. **People evolve when the decisions they own get redrawn.** [Read in Chief Executive Magazine ->](https://chiefexecutive.net/to-survive-ai-your-employees-must-evolve-asap/)

sections.heading: The Apertura
sections.body:
===OPTION_A:observation===
A client called me Tuesday. He had paid for a new workflow platform six months ago. The team had stopped using it. He wanted to know whether to switch vendors or pressure the current one. I asked him to send me the process the software was meant to encode. He could not. Nothing in writing. What existed was a senior operator's version of how things should work, built up over years, stored nowhere. The software was not the problem. The software never had a chance. It was trying to automate a process that did not exist outside one person's head.

sections.heading: The Insight
sections.body:
You bought the platform. You ran the pilot. The team loved the demo. Eight months later, nobody opens it. According to [HR Executive](https://hrexecutive.com/how-hr-can-break-the-tech-regret-cycle/), nearly three-quarters of US small and mid-market businesses are re-evaluating their current tech investments, with poor organizational fit named as the primary cause.

Most advisors call this a change management problem. Send people back to training. Rewrite the rollout plan. Hire a new project lead. That is the wrong diagnosis. The software did not fail because the team resisted it. It failed because the operating model behind it was never written down. You bought a system to automate a process that existed only in senior people's heads. The vendor sold you capabilities. You tried to install them on top of memory.

Here is the reframe. Technology does not create an operating model. Technology encodes the operating model you already have. If the operating model lives only in people, the software has nothing to attach to. The rollout looks like a training failure. It is actually a sequencing failure. Operating Model OS comes before Technology OS. Every time the sequence is inverted, the tech investment becomes the expensive way to discover what the operating model was supposed to be.

What I use with clients is a filter I call the Pre-Buy Readiness Test. Before any material technology purchase, three conditions must be true. First, the process the software is meant to encode exists as a written document. Not a vendor flowchart. A plain description the person running the process would sign their name to. Second, a new hire could run that process from the document without shadowing the senior person for a month. Third, the document names who decides when the process breaks and which decisions never leave the owner.

If any of the three fail, the software will fail after purchase. Not because of vendor quality. Because the check was cut before the artifact existed.

This week, do not start with the next purchase. Start with the last one. Pull the software your team stopped using. Write one page. What process was that tool supposed to encode. What document should have existed first. What part of that process still lives in one person's head. Share the page with the senior person who owns the work. Their edits will tell you where the operating model is missing. That same page is the brief for whatever you buy next. If you cannot write it, you are not buying software. You are buying the invoice for an exercise you have not started.

sections.heading: The Field Report
sections.body:
A [report from HR Executive](https://hrexecutive.com/how-hr-can-break-the-tech-regret-cycle/) this week surfaced the pattern mid-market owners are describing in private. Citing research from Expert Market, nearly three-quarters of US small and mid-sized businesses are re-evaluating their current technology investments. A separate Accenture and Wharton School report, referenced in the same coverage, points to poor organizational fit as the driver of most tech-purchase regret.

The HR Executive analysis frames this as an HR problem: whether the organization has the skills to make the investment work. The framing is useful but incomplete. Skills are downstream. The upstream question is whether the process the software was meant to encode existed in writing before the check was cut.

For US-based owners running $5M to $100M businesses, the operational implication is direct. Q2 is the quarter the 2025 tech purchases start showing up in renewal conversations. The ones getting canceled will tell you where the operating model was never actually installed.

sections.heading: The Tool
sections.body: **The One-Page Pre-Buy Brief** is a document written before any material tech purchase, naming the process to be encoded, who owns it today, and what part of it still lives in someone's head. It matters because the tech regret cycle is a purchase-sequence problem, not a vendor problem. Build it in [Notion](https://www.notion.so) or a single Google Doc. The tool does not matter. The discipline does.

sections.heading: The Compass
sections.body: **Watch for this week:** which of your tech vendor renewals get pushed past the scheduled date. Delayed renewals are the earliest visible signal that the regret cycle is real inside your own P&L. If two or more push in the same week, the operating model behind those purchases is the issue, not the vendors. The conversation to have is with the senior person who owns the process, not the account manager who owns the contract.

sections.heading: The Door
sections.body:

If this is useful to someone in your network, forward it - it takes ten seconds and it's the highest compliment.

When you're ready to work together directly, here is how we start: [link]

esContent.subject: Por qué no pegó su última compra de software
esContent.preheader: El ciclo de arrepentimiento tecnológico es un problema de modelo operativo, no de proveedor.
esContent.thesis: La tecnología no crea el modelo operativo: codifica el que ya existe. Si el modelo solo vive en la cabeza de sus operadores, cada compra de software termina convertida en la factura cara del ejercicio que nunca se hizo antes.

sections.heading: LA SEÑAL
sections.body:
*Esta semana: la mediana empresa está descubriendo que cada compra tecnológica es, en realidad, una prueba del modelo operativo, y la mayoría la está reprobando.*

- **Estrategia:** [Chief Executive](https://chiefexecutive.net/preparing-for-uncertainty-without-slowing-progress/) sostiene que los fabricantes ya tienen las herramientas para navegar la incertidumbre; lo que les falta es un marco de decisión. **La estrategia es la regla que le dice cuál herramienta usar.** [Leer en Chief Executive Magazine ->](https://chiefexecutive.net/preparing-for-uncertainty-without-slowing-progress/)
- **Modelos Operativos:** Según [HR Executive](https://hrexecutive.com/how-hr-can-break-the-tech-regret-cycle/), citando datos de Expert Market, cerca de tres cuartas partes de las pymes estadounidenses reevalúan sus inversiones tecnológicas actuales, con el mal encaje organizacional como causa principal del arrepentimiento. **El modelo operativo es lo que el negocio sabía hacer antes de que llegara el software.** [Leer en HR Executive ->](https://hrexecutive.com/how-hr-can-break-the-tech-regret-cycle/)
- **Tecnología:** El cofundador de Instacart, Apoorva Mehta, lanzó un hedge fund que reemplaza a los portfolio managers fundamentales con agentes de IA, según [Bloomberg](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-24/instacart-co-founder-debuts-hedge-fund-where-ai-calls-the-shots). **La IA reemplaza un puesto cuando ese puesto ya era una decisión repetible.** [Leer en Bloomberg ->](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-24/instacart-co-founder-debuts-hedge-fund-where-ai-calls-the-shots)
- **Capital Humano:** [Chief Executive](https://chiefexecutive.net/to-survive-ai-your-employees-must-evolve-asap/) plantea la era de la IA como un mandato para que los empleados se conviertan en creadores de valor con una lectura amplificada por IA. **Las personas evolucionan cuando se redibujan las decisiones a su cargo.** [Leer en Chief Executive Magazine ->](https://chiefexecutive.net/to-survive-ai-your-employees-must-evolve-asap/)

sections.heading: LA APERTURA
sections.body: Un cliente me llamó el martes. Seis meses atrás había pagado una plataforma nueva de flujo de trabajo. El equipo había dejado de abrirla. Quería saber si cambiaba de proveedor o presionaba al que ya tenía. Le pedí que me mandara el proceso que ese software debía codificar. No lo tenía. Nada por escrito. Lo que existía era la versión de un operador senior, construida durante años, guardada en ningún lado. El software no era el problema. Nunca tuvo oportunidad. Intentaba automatizar un proceso que solo vivía en la cabeza de una persona.

sections.heading: EL INSIGHT
sections.body:
Usted compró la plataforma. Hizo el piloto. Al equipo le encantó la demo. Ocho meses después, nadie la abre. De acuerdo con [HR Executive](https://hrexecutive.com/how-hr-can-break-the-tech-regret-cycle/), cerca de tres cuartas partes de las pymes estadounidenses están reevaluando sus inversiones tecnológicas actuales, y la causa principal que aparece es la falta de encaje organizacional.

La mayoría de los asesores diagnostica esto como un problema de gestión del cambio. Repetir la capacitación. Rehacer el plan de implementación. Contratar un nuevo líder de proyecto. El diagnóstico está equivocado. El software no falló porque el equipo se resistiera. Falló porque el modelo operativo detrás nunca quedó por escrito. Usted compró un sistema para automatizar un proceso que solo vivía en la cabeza de sus operadores senior. El proveedor le vendió capacidades. Usted intentó instalarlas sobre la memoria de alguien.

El reencuadre es este. La tecnología no crea un modelo operativo: codifica el que ya existe. Si ese modelo solo vive en las personas, el software no tiene de dónde agarrarse. La implementación parece un fracaso de capacitación; en realidad es un fracaso de secuencia. Operating Model OS va antes que Technology OS. Cada vez que se invierte ese orden, la inversión tecnológica termina siendo la forma cara de descubrir cuál tendría que haber sido el modelo operativo desde el principio.

Lo que aplico con mis clientes es un filtro al que llamo la prueba previa a la compra. Antes de cualquier adquisición tecnológica relevante, deben cumplirse tres condiciones. Una: el proceso que el software va a codificar existe como documento escrito — no un flowchart del proveedor, sino una descripción en texto plano que el responsable del proceso firmaría como propia. Dos: un nuevo ingreso puede operar ese proceso leyendo el documento, sin pasar un mes a la sombra del operador senior. Tres: el documento nombra quién decide cuándo el proceso se rompe y qué decisiones nunca salen del dueño.

Si alguna de las tres falla, el software va a fallar tras la compra. No por la calidad del proveedor, sino porque el cheque se firmó antes de que existiera el documento.

Esta semana, no empiece por la próxima compra: empiece por la última. Saque el software que el equipo dejó de usar. Escriba una página. ¿Qué proceso debía codificar esa herramienta? ¿Qué documento tendría que haber existido primero? ¿Qué parte de ese proceso sigue viviendo en una sola cabeza? Compártala con el operador senior dueño del trabajo. Las ediciones que haga le van a mostrar dónde falta el modelo operativo. Esa misma página es el brief para lo siguiente que compre. Si no puede escribirla, no está comprando software: está pagando la factura de un ejercicio que todavía no comienza.

sections.heading: EL REPORTE DE CAMPO
sections.body:
Sección: spotlight

[Apple lanzó esta semana su plataforma Business](https://elceo.com/tecnologia/apple-va-por-las-pymes-mexicanas-con-su-plataforma-business/) para entrar al mercado pyme mexicano. La compañía ofrece un ecosistema para administrar dispositivos, distribuir aplicaciones, configurar perfiles por área y estandarizar seguridad desde una sola interfaz. La apuesta va dirigida a un segmento que en México acumula rezagos importantes en digitalización.

El anuncio llega justo cuando las compras tecnológicas de 2025 comienzan a aparecer en las conversaciones de renovación, tanto aquí como en Estados Unidos. Investigación de Expert Market, referida por HR Executive, documenta que cerca de tres cuartas partes de las pymes estadounidenses reevalúan hoy sus inversiones tecnológicas, con el mal encaje organizacional como motor del arrepentimiento. Un estudio de Accenture y la Wharton School, citado en la misma cobertura, apunta en el mismo sentido.

Para el dueño de una empresa de entre 5 y 100 millones de dólares, la señal operativa es clara. Las adquisiciones de Apple Business, Workspace, ERPs o plataformas de flujo de trabajo que terminen canceladas en el segundo trimestre no van a revelar fallas del proveedor: van a revelar dónde el modelo operativo nunca quedó realmente instalado.

sections.heading: LA HERRAMIENTA
sections.body: **The One-Page Pre-Buy Brief** es un documento que se redacta antes de cualquier compra tecnológica relevante: nombra el proceso a codificar, quién lo opera hoy y qué parte sigue guardada en la cabeza de alguien. Importa porque el ciclo de arrepentimiento tecnológico es un problema de secuencia de compra, no un problema de proveedor. Constrúyalo en [Notion](https://www.notion.so) o en un Google Doc. La herramienta es lo de menos. La disciplina, no.

sections.heading: LA BRÚJULA
sections.body: **La señal a seguir esta semana:** cuáles de sus renovaciones con proveedores tecnológicos se postergan más allá de la fecha programada. Una renovación que se retrasa es la señal más temprana de que el ciclo de arrepentimiento ya entró a su P&L. Si dos o más se mueven en la misma semana, el problema está en el modelo operativo detrás de esas compras, no en los proveedores. La conversación es con el operador senior dueño del proceso, no con el ejecutivo de cuenta dueño del contrato.

sections.heading: LA PUERTA
sections.body:
Si algo de este número resonó contigo, respóndeme - leo cada mensaje.
Si le es útil a alguien en tu red, reenvíalo - es el mayor cumplido.
Cuando estés listo para trabajar juntos directamente, así es como empezamos: [link]
